subtopic: Pre-contact and early-contact Tuibun Ohlone history in the Coyote Hills/Lower Alameda Creek area: archaeological record (shellmounds, village sites, seasonal rounds, resource management), ethnohistory, Muwekma Ohlone perspectives and place names.

Spanish missionization (Mission San José) and Mexican rancho land tenure around Coyote Hills: labor drafts, disease and demographic change, secularization, ranching, shifts in Indigenous land use; early American annexation and land claims affecting the marshes and hills.

findings.summary: This source outlines how Mission San José (founded 1797 in Ohlone territory) reshaped Indigenous life in the South/East Bay, the broader labor regime that drew people from far beyond the local Ohlone communities, and the effects of Spanish/Mexican policy on land and people. It documents the mission’s reliance on capture and forced labor of Ohlone and then more distant groups (Miwok, Yokuts, Patwin, Wappo, Nisenan), the severe demographic collapse from disease, and ecological disruption from mission livestock—changes that directly affected estuary-based lifeways like those around today’s Coyote Hills. It also summarizes resistance (including punitive expeditions against Ohlone villages and the Estanislao campaigns), and explains Mexican secularization in 1834: administrators inventoried mission assets and were to distribute lands to Indians, but only five Indian pueblos were actually established. Together, these details help frame how the Coyote Hills area shifted from Indigenous-managed estuary landscapes to mission-controlled labor and grazing systems, and how post-1834 policies set the stage for later Mexican ranchos and, ultimately, American-era land claims.
relevant_facts: Mission San José was established in 1797 in Ohlone territory; nearby missions at San Francisco and Santa Clara (both founded two decades earlier) had already drawn on Ohlone communities.

Indians from Mission Santa Clara were sent to build and work at Mission San José; as missions competed for labor, they ranged farther to capture people, first primarily Ohlone, then Miwok, Yokuts, Patwin, Wappo, and Nisenan.

The Bay Area supported large, diverse Indigenous populations across more than forty tribal groups and multiple languages; estuaries provided rich acorns, game, fish, and shellfish that underpinned local lifeways.
Mission policy forced cultural assimilation: bans on Native languages and ceremonies, compulsory instruction in Christianity, Spanish language and customs, and dietary changes.
Introduced foods (including sugar and milk) caused severe health impacts; within two years in the missions many Indians lost all their teeth, and there were life‑threatening infections and diabetes.

Mission livestock (cows, goats, pigs, chickens) overgrazed and trampled Indigenous-tended landscapes, displacing native fauna and disrupting ecological balance, directly affecting estuary food systems.
European diseases (measles, influenza, smallpox) devastated Native populations, with entire villages wiped out.

Spain’s mission governance plan (initially envisioned as a ten‑year transition) stretched to roughly fifty years due to Indigenous resistance, high mortality, and ongoing military-church expeditions to capture more Indians.

Resistance was frequent: in 1805, Spanish forces retaliated against the Ohlone-speaking Luecha after resisting a capture expedition—killing ten and seizing women, children, and men for Mission San José.

Further resistance included Estanislao’s 1828 campaigns (Yokuts and other allies) against Mission San José, which repelled Spanish forces twice; after a larger third attack, Estanislao secured a pardon but later died in an 1839 epidemic.
Punishments used by missionaries included executions, whippings, shackling, confinement, and wooden collars with metal hooks.

Mexico took control in 1821 and secularized the missions in 1834: civil administrators inventoried property and were to divide land—half to Indians, half to clergy—with common areas tended by Indians.

Despite the plan to convert missions into Indian pueblos, only five such pueblos were established (Flores, Pala, San Dieguito, San Juan Capistrano, San Pasqual), indicating limited realization of land redistribution to Native people.
interesting_annecdotes: Some Native people described the Bay’s estuaries—critical to Coyote Hills lifeways—as “Indian Shopping Malls” for their abundance of food, materials, and medicines.

Yokuts families (many later compelled to Mission San José) used large tule balsa canoes called ay yah—over 35 feet long—with a central cut‑out fishing/swimming hole and a mud-disc hearth area for grilling fish.
Mission dietary changes were so harmful that within two years many Indians lost all their teeth.
After an 1805 resistance in Luecha territory, a Spanish punitive expedition killed ten Luecha and captured 29 people (men, women, and children) taken back to Mission San José for baptism and labor.
Estanislao’s resistance twice forced Spanish retreats before a larger assault led him to return and receive a pardon—he later died in an 1839 epidemic.
additional_topics_to_explore: Which specific Mexican-era ranchos encompassed the Coyote Hills and adjacent marshes after 1834 secularization (e.g., Ex‑Mission San José tracts, Rancho Arroyo de la Alameda, Rancho Potrero de los Cerritos), and how mission herds transitioned to private ranching there.
Land and water use changes in the Coyote Hills estuaries from mission livestock grazing to Mexican ranching (e.g., diking, draining, haying, salt grass pasturage).
Quantitative demographic impacts on Ohlone and other groups attached to Mission San José (mortality rates, population loss over time) and implications for village sites near Coyote Hills.
Patterns of post‑secularization Indigenous labor on regional ranchos connected to former Mission San José lands.
Early American annexation: U.S. Land Commission claims affecting ex‑mission and rancho holdings around the Coyote Hills marshes and hills, and outcomes for Indigenous access and occupancy.
Continuity and claims of contemporary Ohlone communities (e.g., Muwekma Ohlone Tribe) to the Mission San José/Coyote Hills area.
Archaeological and paleoecological evidence for shifts in estuary resource use (tule harvesting, shellfish gathering) from pre‑mission to mission/ranch periods.

findings.summary: An 1860 federal survey plat maps the Mexican-era Rancho Potrero de los Cerritos—very likely encompassing the “little hills” and adjacent baylands near today’s Coyote Hills—and shows how the grant was legally recognized in the early American period. Prepared by Deputy Surveyor William J. Lewis under the U.S. Surveyor General and filed with the U.S. District Court (Northern District) as Land Case 119 ND, it “finally confirmed” title to Thomas Pacheco and Agustín (Augustin) Alviso. The map details drainage, marshes, survey markers, township/section lines, and adjoining ranchos, documenting the formalization of boundaries across the marshes and low hills during the U.S. confirmation of Mexican land grants after annexation.
relevant_facts: Title: Plat of the Rancho Potrero de los Cerritos [Calif.]: finally confirmed to Thomas Pacheco and Augustin Alviso.
Surveyed by William J. Lewis, Deputy Surveyor, under instructions from the U.S. Surveyor General, October–November 1860.
Filed with the U.S. District Court (California: Northern District) as part of Land case 119 ND; noted on verso as filed January 28, 1861.
Claimants named: Tomas (Thomas) Pacheco et al. and Augustin (Agustín) Alviso.
Scale: 1:31,680 (40 chains to 1 inch).
Coordinates noted: approximately W 122°04' / N 37°34'.
Cartography: relief shown by hachures; pen-and-ink and watercolor on tracing cloth; sheet size 47 x 59 cm.
Content shown: drainage (bay/marsh features), survey markers, rancho boundaries, township and section lines, and adjoining ranchos.
Includes a table of boundaries and the Surveyor General’s certifications and seal.
Repository and identifier: UC Berkeley, Bancroft Library; Land Case Map E-261; part of the collection 'Maps of private land grant cases of California.'
Descriptive note on verso: “119, U.S. District Court. The United States vs. Tomas Pacheco, et al. Plat of survey from U.S. Sur. General. Filed January 28, 1861 ...”
interesting_annecdotes: The plat is executed in pen-and-ink and watercolor on tracing cloth, a typical but visually striking medium for mid-19th-century federal land case maps.

A handwritten verso note explicitly ties the map to the litigation caption “The United States vs. Tomas Pacheco, et al.,” underscoring how Mexican-era grants were adjudicated in U.S. courts after annexation.
The Spanish place-name 'Potrero de los Cerritos' (pasture of the little hills) preserves the landscape’s defining low hills and pastoral use within the formal U.S. survey record.
additional_topics_to_explore: Compare this plat with the earlier diseño(s) of Rancho Potrero de los Cerritos listed under 'Similar items' to see how informal Mexican-era sketches were translated into precise U.S. surveys.
Identify and map the 'adjoining ranchos' shown on the plat to reconstruct the broader 19th-century land-tenure mosaic around Coyote Hills and the South Bay marshes.
Research biographies of Tomas/Thomas Pacheco and Agustín/Augustin Alviso and their ties to Mission San José lands and post-secularization ranching.
Locate the Surveyor General’s certification text and any accompanying field notes for boundary calls (arroyos, marsh edges, landmarks) affecting the Coyote Hills wetlands.
Analyze how the drainage and marsh features depicted at 1860 compare with modern shoreline and restored marshes at Coyote Hills.
Review the full case file for Land Case 119 ND to trace the U.S. confirmation process under the Land Act of 1851 and any contests over boundaries or title.
Cross-reference Mission San José’s historic potreros to clarify how secularization shifted Indigenous land use into privatized ranching on this tract.

subtopic: Industrial transformation of the landscape: development of Bay salt production at Newark/Coyote Hills (Leslie Salt to Cargill), levee and pond engineering, transportation links (Dumbarton Cut-off/Bridge, Newark rail spurs), labor conditions, and environmental consequences.

findings.summary: This 2002 background report outlines how South Bay salt-making transformed tidal marshes—especially around Newark and the lower Alameda Creek/Mowry Slough reach bordering Coyote Hills—into an engineered landscape of leveed evaporation ponds. It traces the industry from Indigenous salt harvest, to 19th–20th century consolidation under Leslie Salt, and the 1978 purchase by Cargill. It details the solar-evaporation system (intake, sequential evaporators, “pickle” ponds, crystallizers), seasonal harvest logistics using temporary rail lines, and the Newark processing/bittern storage complex. The report documents the scale of marsh conversion, the persistence of mineral rights and continued production even on refuge lands, and the environmental consequences: loss and fragmentation of tidal wetlands, toxic bittern management challenges, altered hydrology, invasive species risks, predator corridors along levees, and complex, costly levee maintenance. It also frames subsequent restoration planning needs and regulatory constraints on desalination/discharge, highlighting tradeoffs for species that have adapted to salt pond habitats.
relevant_facts: Prior to European settlement, Ohlone people harvested salt from natural South Bay ponds; Spanish missionaries adopted these methods.
Commercial solar salt production on the Bay began in 1854 at Mount Eden (Hayward); by 1868 there were 18 companies, which consolidated into Leslie Salt by the Great Depression.

By the 1930s, almost half of the South Bay’s historical tidal marshes had been converted into salt ponds; at peak, salt ponds covered ~36,000 acres Bay-wide; about 26,000 acres of salt ponds remained in the South Bay as of 2002.
Leslie Salt expanded production in the 1950s and sold ~13,000 acres of South Bay ponds to the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service in the mid-1970s (now part of the SF Bay National Wildlife Refuge).
Cargill purchased Leslie Salt in 1978; it does not own Refuge ponds but retained mineral rights and continues salt production on Refuge lands.
Cargill produces roughly one million tons of salt per year, requiring about 40 million tons of Bay water to start the evaporation process.

South Bay production uses a chain of ponds: eight evaporators that reduce brine volume by ~70%, a ninth high-salinity “pickle” pond (~95% of original volume evaporated), and then crystallizers where sodium chloride precipitates (~40 tons/acre).
Harvest occurs in autumn: crystallizers are drained and machinery collects salt; temporary railroad tracks are laid in the ponds and salt is loaded into hopper cars for washing and processing.

Extremely saline bittern (a by-product) is stored in bittern ponds near processing plants at Newark and Redwood City; bittern is toxic to aquatic wildlife, has had no viable market since 1972, and occupies ~1,600 acres of storage/desalting ponds in the South Bay.

The converted pondscape provides important habitat for many birds (including special-status species such as Western snowy plover, California least tern, California brown pelican, California clapper rail) and for harbor seals; the largest pupping site in the Bay is on Mowry Slough within the South Bay salt pond complex.

Historically (pre-1850s), San Francisco Bay hosted ~200,000 acres of tidal marsh (about 50,000 acres each in the South Bay, North Bay, and Suisun) and ~50,000 acres of mudflats; large areas were later filled, diked, or drained for farms, salt ponds, railways, and roads.

Levees are both critical infrastructure and habitat; their maintenance and reinforcement are major costs and should use designs (gentler slopes, vegetated erosion control) that integrate with the landscape.

Restoration planning must balance retaining some managed pond habitat against restoring tidal marsh; changes can reduce or extirpate species adapted to pond conditions, triggering regulatory oversight.

Invasive smooth cordgrass (Spartina alterniflora) threatens restored marshes by forming dense stands, altering channels, and hybridizing with native cordgrass, with potential harm to clapper rails and salt marsh harvest mice.
Non-native predators (red fox, Norway rat) prey on listed birds; restoration could create new dispersal corridors, necessitating ongoing control.

Desalination/discharge of saline brines requires regulatory limits set by the San Francisco Bay Regional Water Quality Control Board; constraints mean desalination must proceed piecemeal over several years, with decisions on who pays, timing, and which ponds remain in production.
Urbanization and watershed changes have degraded Bay water quality (pesticides, metals, runoff over impervious surfaces); restoring tidal and seasonal wetlands can improve water quality.
interesting_annecdotes: Ohlone harvesters reportedly used willow sticks dipped into brine to collect salt crystals—a method adopted by Spanish missionaries.
The highest-salinity evaporator is colloquially called the “pickle” pond before salt crystallizes.
During the autumn harvest, Cargill lays temporary railroad tracks directly across crystallizer beds to move salt in hopper cars.
Mowry Slough, adjacent to the Newark/Coyote Hills area, hosts the Bay’s largest harbor seal pupping site within the salt pond complex.
additional_topics_to_explore: Detailed history of Leslie Salt’s Newark operations and facility build-out adjacent to Coyote Hills and Mowry Slough.
Engineering evolution of South Bay levees and pond layouts in the Newark/Coyote Hills reach, including flood control interfaces and seismic considerations.
Transportation linkages specific to Newark/Coyote Hills: Dumbarton Cut-off and Bridge, Newark rail spurs, and how rail/road access supported salt logistics.
Labor history and working conditions at Leslie Salt and Cargill in Newark (seasonal work, mechanization impacts, unionization, safety).
Bittern management history at Newark: storage capacity changes, any spill/impact records, and current disposal/desalination technologies.
Case studies of species tradeoffs in nearby pond-to-marsh conversions (effects on snowy plover and avocet vs. marsh species) relevant to Coyote Hills-adjacent ponds.
Regulatory milestones and land transfers affecting Newark-area ponds (mineral rights arrangements, Refuge cooperative agreements).
Mapping the chronology of South Bay tidal marsh loss and partial recovery specifically around Alameda Creek/Mowry Slough and Coyote Hills.

findings.summary: This source outlines the origins, construction, and later decline of the Dumbarton Rail Bridge—the first structure to span San Francisco Bay—which linked Newark to Redwood City as part of a roughly ten-mile rail cutoff built by the Southern Pacific Railroad. Construction activity was underway by 1907, with the bridge’s original construction dated to 1910; it employed significant marine engineering, including tug-towed spans and dredging by stern-wheelers for approaches and trestlework. The bridge handled both freight and commuter trains, cost over $3.5 million, and required more than 100,000 tons of rock to support its weight on the bay bottom; it took years longer than anticipated to complete. Newark hosted major civic celebrations tied to the project, including a lavish groundbreaking and an opening event at the Newark Pavilion. With rail traffic declining and alternatives emerging, the last freight train crossed in 1982; a 1991 proposal sought to restore passenger service connecting Caltrain, ACE, BART, and the Capitol Corridor, which would have required full replacement of the bridge.
relevant_facts: The Dumbarton Rail Bridge was the first structure ever to span San Francisco Bay.
Construction work on the Dumbarton Rail Bridge was underway in 1907, and its original construction dates to 1910.
The project was intended to connect Newark with Redwood City via roughly a ten-mile rail link (the Dumbarton cutoff).
The bridge was built by the Southern Pacific Railroad.
It handled both freight and commuter trains during its operational life.
Erection involved towing completed spans into the Dumbarton Narrows by tugboats.
Old stern-wheelers performed dredging to clear channels for approach spans and trestlework.
The bridge cost over $3.5 million to build.
Over 100,000 tons of rock were placed to support the structure on the bay bottom.
Completion took years longer than originally anticipated.
The groundbreaking ceremony was a major civic event described as the 'party of the century.'
The formal opening celebration was held at the Newark Pavilion grounds.
Declining rail traffic and alternative routes led to abandonment; the last freight train crossed in 1982.

A 1991 proposal sought to reactivate passenger service linking Caltrain on the Peninsula with ACE, BART, and the Capitol Corridor in the East Bay, which would have required complete replacement of the existing bridge.
The bridge has remained an object of interest for urban explorers and historians.
interesting_annecdotes: Completed bridge spans were floated into place by tug through the Dumbarton Narrows, a striking marine construction tactic for its time.
Old stern-wheelers—relics of an earlier maritime era—were repurposed to dredge channels for the bridge approaches and trestlework.
The project’s groundbreaking was billed as the 'party of the century,' underscoring Newark’s civic pride in the rail era.
Newark hosted the opening celebration at the Newark Pavilion, tying a regional infrastructure milestone to a local community venue.
additional_topics_to_explore: Clarify the construction timeline (work noted in 1907 vs. original construction dated to 1910) and key milestones/opening date.
Detail the Dumbarton cutoff alignment, the Newark wye, and Newark-area rail spurs that interfaced with the bridge.
Quantify how the bridge supported Newark’s industrial freight—especially salt production shipments—during peak operations.
Document labor conditions, workforce composition, pay, and safety during 1907–1910 construction and subsequent maintenance.
Assess environmental impacts from dredging and the placement of 100,000+ tons of rock on Bay wetlands near Coyote Hills.
Engineering specifics: span types (e.g., swing/wing span), trestle lengths, foundations, and levee or causeway segments.
Trace post-1982 history: structural deterioration, any damage incidents, ownership, and maintenance status.
Compare and coordinate the rail bridge with the highway Dumbarton Bridge and later proposals for the Dumbarton Rail Corridor.
Regulatory and permitting history for the 1991 (and later) reactivation proposals, including environmental review outcomes.

subtopic: Formation of Coyote Hills Regional Park (1960s): EBRPD acquisition from salt interests, planning and opening timeline, interpretive center and Ohlone village reconstructions, archaeological excavations and NAGPRA/repatriation issues, and associated political debates.

findings.summary: The Clio entry frames Coyote Hills’ 1960s formation around the conversion of a Cold War Nike missile base into a regional park. San Francisco Defense Site SF-37, installed in 1956, was decommissioned in March 1963; the administrative barracks were later repurposed as today’s visitor center, while the launcher area was bulldozed and the control area (two miles away) remains in civilian use. After a mid-1960s Stanford Research Institute sonar project monitoring marine mammals vacated the site, Coyote Hills joined the East Bay Regional Park system in 1967. The park now serves as an education and habitat preserve, with active archaeological work tied to the Ohlone (Tuibun) past, a rare preserved shellmound, and a replica Ohlone village (tule hut, sweat lodge, shade shelter, pit house) available by appointment. Earlier salt production had dominated the surrounding marshlands; in later years, salt-company lands adjacent to the park were sold to conservation agencies, situating Coyote Hills within a broader landscape of reclaimed marsh.
relevant_facts: Coyote Hills was part of San Francisco Defense Site SF-37 (Nike missile system) with three separated components: SF-37A (Administration), SF-37C (Control), and SF-37L (Launcher).
The Nike site was installed in 1956 and decommissioned in March 1963.
The Administrative section’s barracks and other buildings remain and were repurposed as the park’s visitor center and administrative facilities.
The Launcher Area had twelve launchers and magazines for thirty Nike-Ajax surface-to-air missiles; it was bulldozed and filled in.
The Control Area, located two miles from the main site, remains in civilian use by law enforcement.
After decommissioning, the Stanford Research Institute operated a mid-1960s sonar project at the site to monitor marine mammals (including dolphins); the project left in the mid-1960s.
Coyote Hills joined the East Bay Regional Park system in 1967.
Archaeological digs within the park have found ample evidence of a Tuibun (Ohlone) village and are still active.
A preserved Ohlone shellmound is located within Coyote Hills; most of the Bay Area’s 400+ shellmounds were destroyed.
The park features a replica Ohlone village with a tule hut, sweat lodge, shade shelter, and pit house; visits are by special appointment and select holidays.

Salt production began in the area in the 1850s and dominated the marshlands around Coyote Hills; remaining Bay Area salt production later consolidated under Cargill, which sold many marshlands back to conservation agencies (including areas adjacent to Coyote Hills).
Other historic industries in the area included a stone quarry and a dairy farm.
In 2014, the park received a 296-acre adjacent land donation (the largest addition since founding), donated by Wilcox Patterson.
interesting_annecdotes: The visitor center occupies former Army barracks from the site’s Cold War Nike missile base.
Between the Army and the park, Stanford Research Institute ran a sonar project here to study marine mammals—dolphins included.
Coyote Hills preserves one of the Bay Area’s few intact shellmounds; by contrast, the Emeryville shellmound was demolished in 1924 after yielding over 700 burials.
The Nike site’s radar-and-guidance Control Area sits two miles from the administrative/launcher areas and is still used by civilian law enforcement today.
additional_topics_to_explore: Identify the specific salt companies involved in land ownership and any parcels transferred to EBRPD for Coyote Hills in the 1960s (e.g., company records, EBRPD board minutes).
Pin down the planning and opening timeline: dates of site planning, public access opening, and visitor center commissioning after 1967.
Document the design, construction, and community consultation behind the replica Ohlone village; clarify when each structure was built and how Ohlone representatives were involved.

Catalog archaeological investigations at Coyote Hills (project leads, site designations, key findings) and summarize any NAGPRA consultations, repatriations, or reburial efforts relevant to the shellmound or excavated materials.
Assess local political debates in the 1960s surrounding the base conversion, habitat preservation, access, and adjacent salt-pond restoration or development.
Clarify the continued use and jurisdiction of the former Nike Control Area and how that interplays with park operations and visitor access.
Investigate interpretive programming history at the park (when school programs began, evolution of exhibits, changes in archaeological policy and ethics).
Review contemporary and later media coverage (e.g., Fremont Argus archives) on the park’s establishment and early public reception.

subtopic: 21st-century restoration, expansion, and controversies: South Bay Salt Pond Restoration and Don Edwards NWR coordination; Patterson Ranch/Coyote Hills East development battles; tribal consultation and representation; sea-level rise, marsh migration, and management tradeoffs.

findings.summary: EBRPD’s Coyote Hills Restoration and Public Access Project (Patterson Ranch) stems from a major 2014 land donation that expanded Coyote Hills Regional Park by about 25% and protected it from urban development after decades of controversy. The donation—valued at over $14 million and the largest ever received through the Regional Parks Foundation—followed more than 25 years of negotiations led by former General Manager Robert E. Doyle and sustained community opposition to residential proposals by groups such as Friends of Coyote Hills and the Citizens’ Committee to Complete the Refuge. After extensive public outreach, the EBRPD Board in September 2019 certified the project’s Final EIR, adopted CEQA findings (including a Statement of Overriding Considerations) and a Mitigation Monitoring and Reporting Program, and approved a Land Use Plan Amendment. The project restores roughly 170 acres of oak savanna, seasonal wetlands, and riparian habitat, and adds public amenities—parking, picnic areas, restrooms, accessible trails, and wildlife overlooks—opening to the public in Fall 2025.
relevant_facts: In 2014, EBRPD received its largest-ever land donation (via the Regional Parks Foundation) at Patterson Ranch, valued at over $14 million.
The donation secured Coyote Hills Regional Park from the threat of urban development and expanded the park by approximately 25%.
The donation followed more than 25 years of negotiations between former General Manager Robert E. Doyle and the Patterson family.
Friends of Coyote Hills, the Citizens’ Committee to Complete the Refuge, and other local stakeholders actively opposed multiple residential development proposals at Patterson Ranch.
EBRPD conducted extensive public outreach, including several public meetings and two planning workshops in the City of Fremont, to inform a Land Use Plan Amendment for Coyote Hills.

In September 2019, the EBRPD Board certified the Final Environmental Impact Report, adopted CEQA findings and a Statement of Overriding Considerations, approved a Mitigation Monitoring and Reporting Program, and adopted the Land Use Plan Amendment.
The project restores approximately 170 acres of oak savanna, seasonal wetlands, and riparian habitat.
Public access improvements include new parking, picnic areas, restrooms, accessible trails, and wildlife overlooks.
The project opened to the public in Fall 2025.
interesting_annecdotes: The Patterson Ranch gift was the Park District’s largest-ever land donation through the Regional Parks Foundation.
Securing the land required more than 25 years of negotiations led by former EBRPD General Manager Robert E. Doyle.
Grassroots groups—Friends of Coyote Hills and the Citizens’ Committee to Complete the Refuge—played a sustained role in defeating multiple housing proposals on the site.
additional_topics_to_explore: How the Patterson Ranch acquisition and restoration coordinates with the South Bay Salt Pond Restoration Project and the Don Edwards San Francisco Bay National Wildlife Refuge.
Details of tribal consultation and representation during the Land Use Plan Amendment and environmental review processes.
Sea-level rise planning for Coyote Hills East: marsh migration pathways, levee and hydrology management, and habitat tradeoffs identified in the EIR.
Specific mitigation measures and long-term monitoring commitments in the Mitigation Monitoring and Reporting Program.
Funding sources beyond the land donation: grants, bonds, or partnerships that supported restoration and public access improvements.
Outcomes from the Fremont public workshops and how community input shaped facility siting and habitat restoration priorities.
Any ongoing constraints signaled by the CEQA Statement of Overriding Considerations (e.g., significant unavoidable impacts) and how they are being managed.
